title: The Wilton Chronicle
summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan item AJE0623.0001.001, internally identified as CME00147. It encodes Carl Horstmann's 1883 title page and printed pp. 1-112: the 4,986 surviving Middle English verse lines of the work now usually called The Wilton Chronicle, the complete Latin Nomina fundatorum, and only the first seven entries of the following Auctoritates. It omits the remainder of Auctoritates on printed p. 113 as well as Horstmann's German introduction and final remarks. Horstmann transcribed British Library Cotton MS Faustina B III directly and collated his readings against William Henry Black's 1830 first edition. The unique manuscript witness is now ff. 199r-263r for the poem and ff. 263r-264v for the complete adjacent Latin texts; the encoded selection ends on f. 264r. Horstmann's milestones use an older foliation five leaves lower. A complete 577-canvas British Library IIIF surrogate of the whole codex and a complete 136-page scan of Horstmann's volume are cached locally with fixity. The poem occupies current ff. 199r-263r / IIIF canvases 407-535, with the ending and Nomina fundatorum sharing f. 263r / canvas 535; the encoded Auctoritates opening ends on f. 264r / canvas 537. The British Library component record's legacy claim that images are unavailable conflicts with the current parent record and working IIIF service; the current public service governs access while the conflict remains documented.

entities.label: General Corpus repository artifact for CME00147
entities.description: The checked-in XML file in the pinned CME submodule, including TEMPHEAD revision metadata and the encoded Horstmann selection.
notes: The root TEXT language string enm lat ger describes the encoded representation, including Latin appendices and German editorial matter, rather than one uniform medieval work language.
The XML preserves 6,738 italic HI spans from Horstmann's typography; their editorial function requires manuscript collation rather than a single global expansion rule.

entities.label: University of Michigan electronic text AJE0623.0001.001 / CME00147
alternate_labels: S. Editha, sive Chronicon vilodunense im Wiltshire dialekt, aus Ms. Cotton. Faustina B III
entities.description: The U-M Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item encoding Horstmann's title page and text pp. 1-112. The public item identifier is AJE0623.0001.001 while the source XML's IDG, BIBNO, and VID are CME00147.
notes: The U-M catalog places Edith, Saint in an author field. The medieval poem is anonymous; Edith is its subject.

The item is complete relative to Horstmann's title page and pp. 1-112 but selective relative to the complete 1883 volume; it stops after seven Auctoritates entries and omits the continuation on p. 113.

entities.label: The Wilton Chronicle
entities.description: Anonymous Middle English verse hagiography and institutional history composed around 1420 for or in close association with Wilton Abbey. It integrates Wilton's royal foundations and historical memory with the life, relic translation, miracles, and cult of St Edith.

date_statements.label: composition
date_statements.value: around 1420, probably during the reign of Henry V
date_statements.notes: The accompanying founders list ends with Henry V as the reigning king; the normalized range is an inference, not a dated colophon.

notes: Only one manuscript witness is known.
The author is anonymous and closely associated with Wilton. Chaplain, priest, or nun identities remain hypotheses.
The poem is incomplete because an entire twelve-folio gathering was removed after surviving line 1030.

entities.label: Legenda sanctae Edithae
entities.description: The eleventh-century Latin Edith legend associated with Goscelin and treated in modern scholarship as a principal source for the Wilton poem's hagiographic material. The exact recension or physical exemplar used by the Middle English poet has not been identified.
notes: Horstmann questioned whether the short twelve-chapter Vita printed by the Bollandists was identical with the Legenda cited by the poet.

entities.label: Horstmann's 1883 edition of S. Editha
alternate_labels: S. Editha sive Chronicon Vilodunense im Wiltshire Dialekt aus Ms. Cotton. Faustina B III
entities.description: Carl Horstmann's complete scholarly volume, including German introduction, direct transcription of the Cotton witness, extensive critical and source notes, two Latin appendices, remarks, and a second appendix. CME00147 encodes the title page and printed pp. 1-112, stopping partway through Auctoritates, not the complete volume.

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., S. Editha sive Chronicon Vilodunense im Wiltshire Dialekt aus Ms. Cotton. Faustina B III (Heilbronn: Gebr. Henninger, 1883).
bibliographic.title: S. Editha sive Chronicon Vilodunense im Wiltshire Dialekt aus Ms. Cotton. Faustina B III

notes: Horstmann cites Black's 1830 edition as generally correct but repeatedly reports manuscript readings, corrections, erasures, and differences from Black, demonstrating direct recollation rather than derivation from Black alone.
Printed pp. 111-113 contain the Latin Nomina fundatorum and Auctoritates after the poem.

entities.label: Black's 1830 first edition of Chronicon Vilodunense
alternate_labels: Chronicon Vilodunense; sive de vita et miraculis Sanctae Edithae
entities.description: William Henry Black's first printed edition of the unique Cotton text, produced at the expense of Richard Colt Hoare. Horstmann describes it as generally correct and uses it as a collation point while returning to the manuscript.

bibliographic.citation: William Henry Black, ed., Chronicon Vilodunense; sive de vita et miraculis Sanctae Edithae Regis Edgari filiae carmen vetus Anglicum, e codice unico Cottoniano (London: Typis Nicholsianis, 1830).
bibliographic.title: Chronicon Vilodunense; sive de vita et miraculis Sanctae Edithae Regis Edgari filiae carmen vetus Anglicum, e codice unico Cottoniano

notes: A current British Library component bibliography gives 1802, but the inspected Horstmann history, DIMEV, title citation, and library holdings support 1830.
Only 100 copies were reportedly printed; no complete public digital scan was located in the searches recorded here.

entities.label: London, British Library, Cotton MS Faustina B III, ff. 199r-264v
alternate_labels: Cotton MS Faustina B III, ff. 199-280
entities.description: The sole manuscript witness of The Wilton Chronicle in a composite parchment codex. The Middle English poem is now ff. 199r-263r; a Latin Wilton founders account and source list follow on ff. 263r-264v, and the companion Middle English Life of St Æthelthryth follows on ff. 265r-279v. The target component was produced at Wilton in the first quarter of the fifteenth century.

date_statements.label: target component production
date_statements.value: first quarter of the fifteenth century

date_statements.label: probable copying date in modern scholarship

historical_designations: Horstmann's foliation ff. 194-259 for the poem and appendices
holding.notes: Current foliation is five leaves higher than Horstmann's milestones across the target component. The poem is current ff. 199r-263r; Latin appendices current ff. 263r-264v.

physical_description.extent: Composite codex, i + 280 folios; target poem ff. 199r-263r; complete adjacent Latin texts ff. 263r-264v; CME00147's encoded Latin selection ends on f. 264r
notes: The poem has a twelve-folio gathering missing after surviving line 1030, between Horstmann old ff. 205 and 206 / current ff. 210 and 211.
Benskin treats the extant language as mixed and predominantly West Midland, produced by a scribe probably from northern Warwickshire or southern Staffordshire and likely working at Wilton.
The same hand copies the Edith poem, the intervening Latin account, and the Æthelthryth poem; this does not establish autograph status.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete digital facsimile of Horstmann's 1883 volume
entities.description: A complete 136-page scan of a University of Toronto copy, with PDF, OCR text, and Internet Archive item metadata. The CME source selection maps to PDF page 7 for the title page and PDF pages 15-126 for printed pp. 1-112.
entities.reproduction_format: 136-page PDF, OCR text, and item metadata
notes: The complete volume is cached, including the German introduction and material omitted by CME00147.

entities.label: British Library complete IIIF facsimile of Cotton MS Faustina B III
entities.description: The current provider-published 577-canvas color IIIF sequence for the complete codex, from front through spine. The poem occupies current ff. 199r-263r / canvases 407-535; CME00147's encoded Latin selection continues through f. 264r / canvas 537, while the complete adjacent Latin text continues through f. 264v / canvas 538.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation manifest with 577 color image canvases; local bundle requests 568 images at 1800-pixel width and retains 9 native-size fallbacks
notes: Complete means every canvas exposed by the provider manifest when retrieved, including covers, endleaves, blanks, and adjacent works; it does not assert independent audit of unexposed physical surfaces.
The provider manifest states: Public Domain in most countries other than the UK. UK and contractual questions are modeled separately in rights.

entities.label: DIMEV record 421, Almighty God in Trinity
entities.description: The Digital Index of Middle English Verse record for The Life of St Editha, with incipit, explicit, sole-witness locus, current foliation note, IMEV/NIMEV number 243, and Black and Horstmann edition references.

entities.label: Dockray-Miller's 2009 edition and translation
alternate_labels: Saints Edith and Æthelthryth: Princesses, Miracle Workers, and their Late Medieval Audience
entities.description: Modern scholarly edition, facing or accompanying translation, notes, glossary, and study of The Wilton Chronicle and the companion Wilton Life of St Æthelthryth.

bibliographic.citation: Mary Dockray-Miller, ed. and trans., Saints Edith and Æthelthryth: Princesses, Miracle Workers, and their Late Medieval Audience: The Wilton Chronicle and the Wilton Life of St Æthelthryth, Medieval Women: Texts and Contexts 25 (Turnhout: Brepols, 2009).
bibliographic.title: Saints Edith and Æthelthryth: Princesses, Miracle Workers, and their Late Medieval Audience: The Wilton Chronicle and the Wilton Life of St Æthelthryth

notes: This later edition supports interpretation and comparison but is not upstream of the 1883 source encoded by CME00147.

entities.label: Benskin's 1991 dialect and scribal study
entities.description: Michael Benskin's complete article 'In reply to Dr Burton,' especially its analysis of the Cotton Faustina B III Wilton component. It separates likely South Wiltshire originals from a mixed, predominantly West Midland copy and distinguishes author from scribe.

bibliographic.citation: Michael Benskin, 'In reply to Dr Burton,' Leeds Studies in English, new series 22 (1991): 209-262.
bibliographic.title: In reply to Dr Burton

entities.label: Camp's study of Edith and women's history
entities.description: Cynthia Turner Camp's chapter analyzing the poem's reconstruction of Wilton's pre-Conquest past for a female community and its joining of saints' life with history writing.

bibliographic.citation: Cynthia Turner Camp, 'Edith of Wilton and the Writing of Women's History,' in Anglo-Saxon Saints' Lives as History Writing in Late Medieval England (Boydell & Brewer, 2015), 25-63.
bibliographic.title: Edith of Wilton and the Writing of Women's History
bibliographic.container_title: Anglo-Saxon Saints' Lives as History Writing in Late Medieval England

access.access_method: Follow the current British Library Archives and Manuscripts request instructions. The catalog states that a letter of introduction is required and consultation is onsite in London, subject to current conditions.

Submit the online digitisation form with shelfmark Cotton MS Faustina B III and exact folios. The service distinguishes reference JPEGs from reproduction-quality TIFFs and asks users to contact licensing for publication reuse.
access.format: Research JPEG or reproduction-quality TIFF quotation
notes: The public IIIF sequence already provides a complete research facsimile. A paid order may be useful only if higher resolution, color control, or publication-quality files are needed.

The rate card lists 2025/26 standard charges of £15.25 for the first JPEG image and £0.58 for each subsequent JPEG; £27.70 for the first TIFF and £7.25 for each subsequent TIFF; complex imaging is £58.19 per image. Obtain a current quotation before relying on these rates.

rights.component: Repository-specific XML file, markup, and revision metadata
rights.basis: The pinned CME repository contains an Unlicense text framed as applying to software. Its scope over corpus XML, markup, data, and edition-derived typography is not explicit enough to treat the full artifact as unambiguously licensed.
notes: Underlying medieval text, historical editions, manuscript images, and modern scholarship are separately scoped.

rights.component: Electronic text as distributed by the University of Michigan
rights.basis: The current U-M item states that the material is in the public domain and supplies No Copyright-United States status.
The anonymous work was composed around 1420 and is centuries outside copyright term. Modern editions, markup, translations, scholarship, and photographs remain separate components.

rights.component: Fifteenth-century manuscript textual and codicological content
rights.basis: The manuscript content is medieval and centuries outside copyright term. Physical ownership, photography, and provider terms are separate.

rights.component: 1883 edition, transcription, introduction, notes, and typography
rights.basis: The volume was published in 1883 and is outside the maximum United States copyright term; Internet Archive metadata marks the item NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States.

rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions with ordinary author-term protection
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1830 and its nineteenth-century editor died long ago; the work is outside ordinary copyright terms. Repository or reproduction terms for a particular copy remain separate.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan derivatives and item access
rights.basis: Internet Archive item metadata records possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT and copyright-region US.

rights.jurisdiction: Most countries other than the United Kingdom, as asserted by the provider
rights.basis: The IIIF manifest's item-level rights field states Public Domain in most countries other than the UK. The provider does not enumerate every covered jurisdiction, and users remain responsible for their determination.
notes: This is an attributed provider statement, not a Creative Commons license or a universal worldwide determination.
The British Library terms say content specifically marked public domain may be used as desired but place responsibility and risk on the user.

rights.basis: The item-level IIIF statement expressly excludes the UK from its broad public-domain claim. No item-specific UK open license was found.
notes: Ask the British Library for written guidance before redistributing the complete image bundle or using photographs in UK-facing commercial publication.

rights.component: Newly commissioned research or reproduction-quality images
rights.basis: The imaging service applies its own order terms and directs publication users to licensing. A paid image order does not by itself grant redistribution or publication rights.
The versioned source release includes a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 license.

rights.component: Modern edition, translation, notes, glossary, and scholarship
rights.basis: The edition was published in 2009 and is currently sold by its publisher; no open-content license was found.
The complete modern article is publicly downloadable, but no Creative Commons or equivalent open-reuse license was found on the landing page or PDF.
notes: Facts and short attributed analysis may be cited; public download is not treated as permission to republish the article wholesale.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Complete Horstmann volume with title page, German introduction pp. III-VIII, text pp. 1-113, and remarks/Appendix II pp. 114-116
editorial_practices.representation: CME00147 encodes the title page and text pp. 1-112 but omits the introduction, the Auctoritates continuation on p. 113, and printed pp. 114-116.
editorial_practices.description: The electronic item is complete for the surviving Middle English poem and Nomina fundatorum but includes only the first seven of fourteen Auctoritates entries and is selective relative to the complete source edition.
editorial_practices.scope: Entire U-M item relative to Horstmann 1883

editorial_practices.source_feature: Cotton manuscript readings and Black's 1830 first edition
editorial_practices.representation: Horstmann transcribes the manuscript directly, describes Black as generally correct, and repeatedly records manuscript corrections, erasures, uncertain readings, and differences marked Bl. in the apparatus.
editorial_practices.description: Black is a comparison edition, while Cotton Faustina B III remains Horstmann's base source.
editorial_practices.scope: Poem and associated Latin appendices, especially the poem's critical notes

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript abbreviation, correction, erasure, and supplied-letter phenomena
editorial_practices.representation: Horstmann uses extensive italic letter sequences within words; CME00147 preserves 6,738 such spans as HI REND='italic'. Parentheses, superscripts, and notes separately record other interventions.
editorial_practices.description: Many italic sequences visibly function as expansions or supplied letters, but the complete system is not reduced to one meaning because some nearby apparatus concerns corrections and overwritten readings. Each sequence should be checked against the manuscript facsimile before use as diplomatic evidence.
editorial_practices.scope: The full poem and encoded Latin selection as printed on pp. 1-112
notes: This practice records the dominant function suggested by the typography without asserting that every italic span is an abbreviation expansion.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Horstmann's italic typography inside the transcription
editorial_practices.representation: The encoding wraps the italic sequences in 6,738 HI elements with REND='italic' rather than silently normalizing them.
editorial_practices.description: The XML preserves an edition-level typographic signal. It does not independently verify every expanded or supplied letter against the manuscript.

evidence.citation: CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00147.xml at pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for file identity, encoded source selection, work and appendix boundaries, line numbering, languages, folio and page milestones, notes, lacuna, and editorial typography.

evidence.citation: Local XPath structural audit of CME00147.xml.
evidence.summary: Confirmed IDG, BIBNO, and VID CME00147; TEXT languages enm lat ger; 3 DIV1, 5 DIV2, 6 HEAD, 46 P, 113 PB, 4,986 L, 3 LG, 347 NOTE, 65 MILESTONE, 7 ITEM, 1 LIST, 6,738 italic HI, 396 SUP, and 2 TAILNOTE elements. The three poem segments contain 1,030, 1,361, and 2,595 lines; the Latin addendum has 33 Nomina fundatorum paragraphs and one Auctoritates paragraph containing seven entries. The final PB is printed p. 112; no p. 113 milestone or its remaining seven authorities is encoded.

evidence.citation: CME/LICENSE at pinned commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Contains the Unlicense text framed as applying to software; it does not explicitly resolve scope over XML, markup, data, historical editions, or manuscript images.

evidence.summary: Official publisher description for the modern edition and translation, conventional title The Wilton Chronicle, Wilton and early-fifteenth-century composition, bibliographic identifiers, contents, and current purchase access.

evidence.citation: Mechthild Gretsch, review of Saints Edith and Æthelthryth, English Historical Review 126, no. 518 (2011): 121-122.
evidence.summary: Independent scholarly review describing the poem's historical opening, the Edith life after the manuscript loss, likely early-fifteenth-century date, possible common authorship with the Æthelthryth poem, Wilton connection, and manuscript production around 1425.

evidence.citation: The Medieval Review, review of Mary Dockray-Miller, Saints Edith and Æthelthryth (2009).
evidence.summary: Summarizes the modern edition and argument that the poet relied primarily on the eleventh-century Goscelin-associated Legenda Edithae while reshaping it for Wilton, women readers, and joined historical-hagiographic purposes.

evidence.citation: Michael Benskin, 'In reply to Dr Burton,' Leeds Studies in English, new series 22 (1991): 209-262.
evidence.summary: Separates likely South Wiltshire originals from the mixed, predominantly West Midland extant copy; localizes the scribe toward northern Warwickshire or southern Staffordshire and probably Wilton; and distinguishes author, scribe, and institutional setting.
notes: The hash is for the complete locally cached 55-page article PDF.

evidence.citation: Cynthia Turner Camp, 'Edith of Wilton and the Writing of Women's History,' in Anglo-Saxon Saints' Lives as History Writing in Late Medieval England (Boydell & Brewer, 2015), 25-63.
evidence.summary: Frames the poem as an anonymous work composed around 1420 for an identifiable female community, reconstructing Wilton's pre-Conquest past through joined hagiography and history writing.

evidence.citation: University of Leeds Special Collections, participant/holding result for William Henry Black and Chronicon Vilodunense (1830).
evidence.summary: Reports a Leeds Archive Print holding of Black's 1830 edition at Large Modern History Y-3.1/WIL, providing a request or onsite lead when no public digital copy was located.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive advanced search for Chronicon Vilodunense and 1830, supplemented by title, editor, and imprint searches.
evidence.summary: No complete public digital copy of Black's 1830 edition was located. The search did locate Horstmann's later volume and catalog references, so the result is recorded as no public copy found rather than unavailable or lost.

open_questions.question: Does a complete public digital facsimile of William Henry Black's scarce 1830 edition exist outside the repositories searched?
next_steps: Ask University of Leeds Special Collections about photography or digitisation of Large Modern History Y-3.1/WIL.
Search Google Books, HathiTrust, British Library, Bodleian, Salisbury Cathedral, and WorldCat holdings by the full Latin title and imprint.
Cache the complete edition if a lawful direct download becomes available.
notes: The edition is extant and requestable through holdings; no-public-copy-found is only a digital-access finding.

open_questions.question: What exact editorial function does each of Horstmann's 6,738 italicized letter spans serve?
next_steps: Collate each HI span against the complete IIIF manuscript bundle and the 1883 page image.
Locate an explicit typographic key in Horstmann's related editions, correspondence, or publisher matter.
Record expansions, supplied readings, manuscript corrections, and editorial conjectures separately if a machine-actionable collation is created.
notes: The available facsimile makes this research practical, but the present lineage does not equate every italic span with one kind of elision.

open_questions.question: Which recension and physical exemplar of the Latin Edith legend underlies the Middle English poem?
next_steps: Compare Dockray-Miller's full source apparatus with Horstmann's objections to identifying the named Legenda with the short Bollandist Vita.
Do not create a direct physical-exemplar relation without manuscript or edition evidence.

open_questions.question: What written reuse determination does the British Library apply to redistribution or publication of these IIIF images in the United Kingdom and in a commercial print-on-demand context?
next_steps: Ask licensing@bl.uk for an item-specific written determination, quoting the IIIF statement Public Domain in most countries other than the UK.
Keep the complete image bundle Git-ignored until redistribution rights are clear.
Separate use of public-domain medieval text and factual collation from republication of the image files.

open_questions.question: Are digital images of Cotton MS Faustina B III currently available despite the component catalog's legacy unavailability statement?
next_steps: Use the parent record's current full-digital-coverage link and working IIIF manifest for access.
Retain the component's contrary sentence as historical catalog evidence until the British Library updates it.
notes: All 577 provider canvases were successfully cached and the ZIP passed integrity validation.

review_notes: Current British Library foliation governs discovery and IIIF navigation; Horstmann's old foliation remains attached to edition and XML milestones.
Horstmann's relation to Black is modeled as direct manuscript transcription plus collation, not as an edition copied from Black.
The abstract poem, the two adjacent Latin texts, the physical codex, historical editions, U-M encoding, repository artifact, and digital photographs remain separate layers.

CME00147 stops on printed p. 112 / current f. 264r after seven Auctoritates entries; the complete source list continues for seven more entries on p. 113 / f. 264v and is present in the full cached sources but not attributed to the encoding.
The complete codex and complete Horstmann volume are cached; work_portion locators associate the target without cropping the source objects.
The Benskin article cache is a supporting scholarly source with no open redistribution license located; it remains Git-ignored.
The British Library image-rights record preserves the provider's exact territorial caveat and does not convert it into a universal open license.
